title: Electrical detection of spin-flip transition in metal/Na5Co15.5Te6O36 heterostructure
authors: Hirotsugu Tagami, Takuya Kawada, Yuki Shiomi
abstract: We report on the longitudinal magnetoresistance (MR) in thin metal films on an Ising-type antiferromagnetic insulator, Na5Co15.5Te6O36 (NCTO). Steep changes in the MR spectra with hysteresis were observed at spin-flip transitions driven by magnetic fields applied along the easy axis of the NCTO crystal. The MR jumps almost follow step-like changes in magnetization at the spin-flip transition. At very low temperatures where Co moments are partially frozen, the MR anomalies exhibit a tunnel-magnetoresistance-like shape. The observed MR anomalies at the spin-flip transition are attributed to strain effects via magnetostriction upon the magnetic-structure change of the Co nets in NCTO, because similar MR jumps are observed in both Pt/NCTO and Cu/NCTO. Interestingly, we found that the high-field slopes of the MR spectra show opposite signs between Pt/NCTO and Cu/NCTO at low temperatures. Because the opposite signs of the high-field MR are prominent below the antiferromagnetic transition temperature of NCTO, the interaction between the interface spin accumulation and magnetization is likely to contribute to the MR effect in the induced ferromagnetic state.
summary: The researchers investigated the origin of magnetoresistance anomalies in Pt/NCTO and Cu/NCTO heterostructures during magnetic spin-flip transitions. They found that the primary cause of the resistance jumps is strain induced by magnetostriction, though interface spin accumulation contributes to the high-field behavior. This work provides a method for the electrical detection of magnetic transitions in Ising-type antiferromagnets.
main_problem: Determining whether magnetoresistance (MR) anomalies at spin-flip transitions in metal/magnetic insulator heterostructures are driven by interface spin accumulation or by magnetostriction-induced strain.
main_result: The MR anomalies at the spin-flip transition are attributed to magnetostriction-induced strain, while the high-field MR behavior is driven by the interaction between interface spin accumulation and induced magnetization.
method: Longitudinal MR measurements and SQUID magnetometry were performed on Pt and Cu thin films deposited on Na5Co15.5Te6O36 (NCTO) single crystals.
model_or_system: The system consists of Pt and Cu thin films (8.3 nm) on a single-crystal Ising-type antiferromagnetic insulator, Na5Co15.5Te6O36 (NCTO), which features Co ion sublattices and undergoes transitions to partially disordered antiferromagnetic states.
key_observables: Longitudinal magnetoresistance (MR), magnetization (M), magnetic susceptibility (chi), and magnetostriction coefficient (lambda).
important_parameters: Neel temperature (TN approx 50 K), transition to frozen PDA state (approx 20 K), and magnetic field strength (up to 9 T).
main_assumptions: The study assumes that the similarity in MR jumps between Pt and Cu (which have different spin-orbit interactions) implies a strain-based mechanism for the spin-flip transition anomalies.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the crystal structure of NCTO, including the Co(1) triangular networks and zigzag patterns.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the heterostructure and the scientific problem, describes the experimental setup and material properties, presents MR and magnetization results, analyzes the physical mechanisms (strain vs. spin-current), and concludes with implications for antiferromagnetic spintronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Crystal structure of NCTO projected in the ab plane. Three Co ion sublattices: Co(1) (blue), Co(2) (green), and Co(3) (cyan). Co(2) and Co(3) are located at the center of the triangle formed by Co(1) (b) Zigzag chain of Co(1) (blue) along the c-axis. (c) Alternate arrangement of Co(2) (green) and Co(3) (cyan) along the c-axis. Co(3) site is partially replaced by Na ions (yellow). (d) Magnetic structure of the Co(1) triangle at low temperatures at zero field [10]. (e) Powder X-ray diffraction pattern of NCTO at room temperature. The red open circles represent the experimental data, and the blue lines represent the simulation results. The inset is a photograph of an NCTO single...

FIG. 2. Magnetic field (H) dependence of (a),(c) magnetization (M) for an NCTO single crystal and (b),(d) longitudinal MR spectra of a Pt/NCTO bilayer at (a),(b) high temperatures (≥10 K) and (c),(d) low temperatures (≤5 K), respectively. The MR ratio is defined as MR = {ρ(H) −ρ(0)}/ρ(0), where ρ(H) is the resistivity at H. In all panels, H was applied parallel to the c-axis. Solid and dotted curves represent data obtained during field-decreasing (from 9 T to −9 T) and field-increasing (from −9 T to 9 T) scans, respectively.

FIG. 3. Magnetic field (H) dependence of the MR ratio of Pt/NCTO (blue) and Cu/NCTO (red) at (a) 5 K and (b) 10 K. The MR ratio is determined by MR= {ρ(H) −ρ(0)}/ρ(0), where ρ(H) is the resistivity at H. The data for Pt/NCTO repeat the ones from Fig. 2 (b) and (d).

FIG. 4. Temperature (T) dependence of the slope of MR spectra at high fields (µ0|H| ≥7 T) for Pt/NCTO (blue) and Cu/NCTO (red). The plotted data are the averaged results obtained from four field sweeps (9 →7 T, −7 →−9 T, −9 → −7 T, and 7 →9 T), where the sign of the negative field data is inverted. Error bars indicate the standard deviation in the linear fit.
